🌍 Site Location & Climate — Atmosphere • Water Vapour Density • ITU-R P.837-8 Rain Rate Standard atmosphere (ITU-R P.835)
💡 How to create a link — click to expand
  1. Pin Site A — type a city in the Site A search box, or click the map (first click = Site A)
  2. Pin Site B — type in Site B search box, or click the map again (second click = Site B)
  3. The link line and distance appear automatically and are applied to the link budget
  4. Set antenna heights (m AGL) for each site — default is 3 m
  5. Click ↻ Refresh to fetch terrain elevation and draw the LOS profile with Fresnel zone
  6. Use ▲ Optimise Heights to auto-calculate minimum heights for 60% Fresnel clearance
  7. Click 🌐 Fetch Climate (Site A) to pull temperature, pressure, WVD and rain rate
  8. Click ✅ Apply to Link Budget in the results panel to use the site climate data
📍 You can also type lat/lon directly.   Use "Zoom to:" to jump the map to a country or region.   Click ✕ Clear Link to start over.

🔴 Capacity vs Availability
Scroll=zoom   Drag=pan
Each point = actual availability of that modulation at current distance — Scroll to zoom in on high-modulation area
📈 Distance vs Availability
Select modulations:
X: distance (km, 0.1 km steps) — Y: availability % (log-outage "nines" scale) — Red horizontal = target — Navy vertical = current distance
🔗 Multi-Band Aggregation — Capacity vs Availability
Band Frequency BW Pol Gt / Gr FSL Rain A0.01 Top mod Top capacity Outage @ top Availability @ top
X = link unavailability (log "nines" scale) — Y = throughput (Mbps). Curves: Band A staircase, Band B staircase, Aggregate (sum). Higher availability target → lower aggregate capacity (mmW falls out first).  ·  Scroll = zoom   Drag = pan

End A: —
End B: —
💡 Quick-Link (default): click one site → rubber-band follows the cursor → click another site to instantly create a draft link. Each connection advances the rubber-band to the latest clicked site, so successive clicks build a chain A→B→C→D naturally. End the chain with Esc, a left-click on empty map, or a right-click anywhere. Right-click on a site marker also opens its info popup (coords + type picker). Then double-click any draft line on the map to design it (configure radio in Single-Link → Add Current Link upgrades the draft).

Distance-banded selection rules + global engineering defaults, used to auto-plan links from their geographic distance.

📍 Distance-Banded Planning Rules
# From (km) To (km) Band Product Frequency
(GHz)		 Channel BW
(MHz)		 Target Capacity (Mbps) → Auto (cfg / mod) Target Availability
(%)		 Prot. SD
🔧 Global Defaults
💡 How these are used: when you design a link (from the Network view or via the auto-planning hook), the tool looks up the row whose From ≤ link distance < To and pre-selects that row's band, product, channel BW, target capacity and target availability. The global defaults are then applied to every link calc. From the matching rule and the link distance the auto-planner picks the appropriate modulation, configuration and antenna size. (Auto-planning consumer code is on the roadmap; this tab is the data source it will read.)

💾 Storage: changes auto-save to this browser's local storage. To share defaults across machines or teammates, a Save to file / Load from file pair is on the roadmap.
📡 Help ITU-R P.530-17 / P.530-18 / P.838-3 / P.676-12 / P.837-8

Overview Quick start Network planning LOS Check Antenna RPE Library Radio File Library Interference Analysis Radio MW settings Antennas Path & Rain Site & Climate Climate Explorer Defaults / Auto cfg+mod Network Report Space Diversity XPD / XPIC BTA Multi-Band Aggregation Reading results Tables & charts Export & theme Methodology Glossary Revision history

📊 What this tool does

This is a point-to-point link planning calculator covering both Microwave (MW) bands (4–42 GHz) and millimetre-wave (mmW) bands (E / W / D-band, 71–174 GHz). Enter the radio configuration, antennas, path length and climate; it computes the full link budget, rain and multipath outage, predicted availability and capacity for every supported modulation profile. Real terrain and climate data can be pulled from the map to auto-size antenna heights and rain rate.

All propagation maths follows the ITU-R Recommendations listed in the header. RSL / TX / capacity tables are Ceragon equipment figures. Results are planning estimates — always confirm against the official datasheet and a field survey before deployment.

⚡ Quick start

  • 1. Choose mode. The Band selector at the top determines the mode: Microwave = 4–42 GHz products; E / W / D-Band = mmW products. Microwave is listed first.
  • 2. Pick the product. For MW select product (IP-20C, IP-50C/CX, RFU-D/D-HP …), frequency band, channel BW and configuration. For mmW select band, product variant and channel BW.
  • 3. Choose antennas. Set a dish size for Site A and Site B.
  • 4. Set the path. Enter the distance (or pin two sites on the map) and rain zone / rate.
  • 5. Set your target. Choose the availability you must meet (e.g. 99.995 %).
  • 6. Read the result. The status box and modulation table show whether the link meets the target and at which modulation, with achievable capacity.
  • 7. (Optional) Multi-band. To aggregate an MW carrier with an E / W / D-band carrier on the same hop, expand the 🔗 Multi-Band Aggregation card at the bottom of the input panel, enable it, and configure Band B. The aggregate capacity-vs-availability chart and headline tiles appear below the main results.
Don't know the distance or rain rate? Use the Site Location & Climate map to pin both ends — distance, terrain profile and ITU-R rain rate are filled in automatically.

🌐 Network planning

The Network tab (top of the page) is for planning a whole network of links rather than one at a time. The same Leaflet map is shared with Single-Link view; switching tabs swaps the workspace but keeps the map context.

Loading sites onto the map

  • Bulk import from Excel or CSV. Click 📥 Import Sites in the network toolbar. The expected columns are Site name, Latitude, Longitude, optional Altitude (m), optional Notes. Header row is auto-detected with common aliases. Coordinates can be decimal degrees (5.949444) or DMS with hemisphere letters (05 56 58 N). A preview modal shows every row colour-coded valid / skipped — review and click Import to commit.
  • Sites template. Click 📎 Sites Template to download a ready-to-fill .xlsx with the correct headers and sample rows.
  • Manual add. Click anywhere on the network map to drop a site; an editable name is prompted.

Bulk import of links (site pairs)

  • Import Links from Excel or CSV. Click 📥 Import Links in the Network toolbar's File ▾ menu. Each row defines a link between two sites: Site name S1, Site name S2, Latitude S1, Latitude S2, Longitude S1, Longitude S2. Header row is auto-detected with common aliases (S1/S2, 1/2, A/B all accepted). Coordinates may be decimal degrees or DMS with hemisphere letters — same parser as Import Sites.
  • Site resolution per row. If a site name already exists in the network, it is reused (its existing coordinates win — the row's lat/lon for that side are ignored). If it doesn't exist, it is auto-created from the row's coordinates as a Generic site. The preview modal labels each side per row: ✓ existing, ⥁ from earlier row, or ✚ new site.
  • Links are added as drafts. Same shape as KML-imported or Quick-Link draft links: gray dashed line on the map, ready to be designed by double-clicking the line (configure radio in the LBC view, then Add Current Link upgrades it from draft to designed). Self-loops (S1 = S2) and duplicates of links already in the network are skipped with a clear reason.
  • Links template. Click 📎 Links Template to download a ready-to-fill .xlsx with the six required headers and example rows in both DMS and decimal formats.

Creating links — Quick-Link (default)

  • Two clicks per link. Left-click any site marker on the map — it stages as End A and a dashed amber rubber-band line follows the cursor. Left-click another marker — the draft link is created on the spot. No popup, no buttons, no extra steps.
  • Chains build automatically. After each connection the rubber-band advances to the just-clicked site as the new End A, so successive clicks build a chain A→B→C→D naturally. For a hub-and-spoke fan-out, end the chain after each spoke and start a new one from the hub.
  • Ending a chain — three ways: Esc, a left-click on empty map, or a right-click anywhere on the map. The rubber-band disappears; the next left-click on any site starts a new chain (or branch) from that site as the new End A.
  • Right-click on a marker opens the site info popup (coordinates + Site Type picker) and ends any pending chain in the same gesture.
  • Classic flow. Toggle Quick-Link OFF in the Selection side panel to get explicit Use as End A / Use as End B buttons in the marker popup. The Selection panel's ➕ Add Connection (draft) button is the manual two-step path; Chain mode toggle governs whether End B becomes the new End A after each manual Add.

Site type / icon library

Each network site can be tagged with a type. Right-click any marker to open the site info popup — the type picker is a 4×2 grid above the coordinates:

  • Generic (teardrop / teal) · Hub (hexagon / purple) · Repeater (triangle / orange) · Antenna (circle / sky)
  • Customer (square / green) · POP/BTS (diamond / red) · Important (star / amber) · Building (house / slate)

The chosen shape and colour show on the map marker and as a small inline swatch next to the site's name in the Sites table, so the table mirrors the map at a glance. Selection state (End A / End B during Quick-Link) overrides with the canonical blue / red and an amber outline ring — site type and selection are both legible simultaneously. Icons are persisted in the .lbcnet.json save file; older save files without icon data load with every site defaulting to Generic.

Designing & opening existing links

  • Double-click a link line on the map → opens the design in Single-Link view. For a draft, the pair is pre-filled and you can start designing; for a designed link, the full radio configuration and results are restored exactly as saved.
  • Single-click a link line → highlights the link and its row in the Links table (no popup).
  • Sites & Links tables next to the map are sortable. The Links table doubles as a design index: click a row to highlight on the map and fit the view to the link, double-click to load. Each row supports rename / zoom-to / delete.

Line colours at a glance

Line styleMeaning
Dashed grayDraft — connectivity only, no radio design yet.
Solid blueDesigned, OK — achieved availability meets the target.
Solid amberDesigned, Warn — below target but ≥ 99 %.
Solid redDesigned, Fail — below 99 %.

Map declutter & basemap controls

  • 🗺️ Map dropdown in the Network toolbar — picks the basemap from Street (OSM), Satellite (Esri), Terrain (OpenTopoMap), HOT (OSM Humanitarian) or Blank (no tiles, for fast pan/zoom on large networks; a faint theme-aware grid replaces the tiles). The same dropdown lives in Single-Link view and stays in sync.
  • 📏 Link Labels: ON/OFF — permanent label at each link's midpoint showing the distance (km in metric mode, mi in FCC).
  • 📛 Site Names: ON/OFF — hides the white-boxed name labels under every map marker. Useful when sites cluster tightly and labels start overlapping. The markers themselves (the SVG pin) stay visible; the Sites table and right-click popups are unaffected.

Saving & loading the network

  • File > Save Network. On Chromium browsers (Chrome / Edge / Opera / Brave) the OS native Save dialog opens — pick the folder AND filename. The project name is taken from the filename you type. Subsequent saves silently overwrite the same file at the same location.
  • File > Load Network. Pick a .lbcnet.json file. The file handle is remembered, so Load → edit → Save overwrites the loaded file in place.
  • File > Save Network As… Always prompts for a new location — for saving a copy or fork without changing the project name.
  • Project name appears in the network toolbar as a 📁 ProjectName badge and is stored inside the .lbcnet.json payload (round-trips through Save / Load).
  • Firefox / Safari: a single name prompt + download to the Downloads folder.

Round trip: Network ↔ Single-Link

  • Whenever you arrive at Single-Link from a network action, a blue context banner appears at the top of the left input panel showing the link being designed. Two buttons: ✓ Save to Network commits the design (upgrades drafts silently, prompts before overwriting an existing designed link); ✕ Cancel discards and returns to network.
  • If results haven't computed yet (terrain still loading), the save asks to confirm or wait.
  • A pulsing yellow dot appears on the Network tab whenever Single-Link has unsaved edits. Manually switching tabs or closing the browser warns first.
  • From Single-Link standalone (not arrived from network), + Add Current Link in the toolbar pushes the current design into the network as a new link.
Tip for large networks: drop all sites first, sketch the entire topology with Quick-Link (just click site → site → site → … building a chain in one continuous gesture), then come back and design each leg by double-clicking the dashed lines. The colour of each line tells you which still need attention. For dense maps, toggle Site Names OFF to declutter once you know the layout.

📡 LOS Check (Network Designer)

Three modes for vetting line-of-sight between sites before committing to a link design. All three use the same engine: ITU-R standard K = 4/3 atmospheric refraction, 60% Fresnel-1 clearance, Open-Meteo Copernicus 90 m DEM with per-coordinate caching. Each site contributes the antenna height stored on the site (antAlt); when a site has no antenna height set the tool falls back to a configurable default (30 m AGL).

ModeHow to use
From selected site (radius)Click a site first (it becomes the source). Open 📡 LOS Check → pick this mode → enter radius, frequency, default antenna height. Tool checks LOS from the source to every other site whose distance is ≤ the radius.
All-to-all within radiusOpen the menu → pick this mode → enter radius / frequency / antenna height. Every pair of sites whose mutual distance is ≤ the radius gets a check. For N sites in radius that's N×(N−1)/2 paths — use the Cancel button on the progress modal to stop early.
By map-tile selectionActivates a tile grid at the current map zoom. Only tiles that contain at least one site are drawn (capped at 250; zoom out if the cap trips and the tiles will merge to larger ones). Each tile shows a site-count badge. Click tiles to toggle selection (green fill = selected). The floating panel summarises the running count and has a ⚙ 7 GHz · 30 m button to set frequency + antenna height for the run. ▶ Run LOS runs all-to-all on the sites inside the selection.

Results card mounts under the toolbar (same pattern as Auto-Plan). Counts at the top — ✓ clear · ⚠ marginal · ✗ obstructed. The per-row table shows pair, distance, status, worst 60% F1 clearance, worst geometric clearance, and a per-row + Draft link button (disabled when the link already exists or the path is obstructed). A + Create all draft links button at the top creates drafts from every clear+marginal pair in one click.

Map preview — colour-graded polylines overlay the map for every checked pair: green / solid for clear, amber / dashed for marginal, red / dashed for obstructed. Hover any line for a tooltip with distance, status, worst Fresnel clearance, worst geometric clearance.

Cancellation — Esc or a right-click anywhere on the map dismisses whatever LOS state is active (tile-selection overlay first, otherwise the results+preview lines). The Dismiss button on the results card has the same effect.

Status interpretation: clear = line of sight is above the 60% Fresnel zone (usable for RF); marginal = line clears the terrain but the Fresnel zone grazes (workable with margin loss but not ideal); obstructed = even the pure geometric line is blocked by terrain (no LOS).

📡 Antenna RPE Library (v8.1)

A standards-compliant antenna pattern importer + polar viewer. Imports antenna radiation pattern files in NSMA WG 16.99.050 format and feeds the interference engine with the off-axis discrimination values it needs to compute C/I. Open it from File ▾ → 📡 Antenna RPE Library; it opens as a floating, resizable, draggable window so it can stay open alongside the network map.

FeatureWhat it does
Dual-format parserAccepts both NSMA (the classical bare-key format with HH N / VV N section headers, used by many vendors) and ADF (CSV-based KEY:,VALUE form with PATCUT:,AZ sections, Andrew CommScope variant). Auto-detection chooses the right parser by sniffing the file content — both work with any of the .txt / .nsma / .adf / .ant extensions.
Import pathsThree ways to import: + Pick file(s) for a multi-select dialog (case-insensitive extension matching); + Pick folder walks a whole tree recursively, picking up every matching file; drag-and-drop accepts either flat file drops or full-folder drops (Chromium/Edge expose folder entries via webkitGetAsEntry).
Polar viewerClick any row to see the four polarisation patterns (HH, HV, VV, VH) overlaid on a polar plot, with peak gain, 3 dB beamwidth, F/B ratio, estimated dish size from gain, and per-pair envelope statistics.
Filter stripManufacturer + frequency band + size dropdowns combined with a text search via AND. Counts update live as the filter changes.
StoragePersisted to localStorage under ceragon_rpe_library. Survives reload; same library is available to every project in the browser. Floating-window position + size remembered per session in lbc_window_rpeModal.
Drop your vendor catalogue (e.g. Andrew/CommScope .adf exports, RFS NSMA exports) into the window once and it's available to every link in every project. The interference engine reads the patterns directly — you never touch the library after import.

📻 Radio File Library (v8.1)

Importer for Pathloss V5 .raf radio specification files. Each file holds the full radio data for one (product, band, channel BW) combo — Tx power and ATPC range, Rx thresholds per modulation, per-modulation C/I and XPIF, the 81-point Tx spectral mask, the 81-point Rx selectivity curve, and the 27-point T/I (NFD) curve. The interference engine reads these directly when computing co-channel and adjacent-channel rejection.

FeatureWhat it does
Spec-type badgeEvery imported radio is tagged Guarantee (conservative datasheet values; the published spec), Typical (measured / realistic), or Typical (derived) — explained below.
🧪 Derive TypicalOne-click generator. For every Guaranteed entry that doesn't have a paired Typical, creates a derived Typical by subtracting the per-band NF delta (typical NF − worst-case NF) from every Rx threshold. NF deltas come from an embedded table covering all 5 NF families × standard bands. Mask, selectivity, T/I, C/I, XPIF, Tx power are unchanged. Use this when only Guaranteed RAFs are available from R&D and you need realistic numbers for interference analysis.
Product matcherAuto-detects the specific Ceragon product from the radio ID, per the Pathloss naming convention documented in Pathloss_Radio_Files_format_for_LBC. Resolves IP20C- → IP-20C, IP20C-HP- → IP-20C-HP, IP20D-D{n} → RFU-D, IP20D-HP{n} → RFU-D-HP, IP50EX / IP50EXA → IP-50EX(A), IP50EXP → IP-50EX-P, IP50EXAP → IP-50EXA-P, and the standard IP-50C / IP-50CX / IP-50E variants. Used by the Product type filter and by Auto-bind to find compatible radios for each link.
Detail viewerSelecting a radio shows ADMOD parameters, the Tx spectral mask overlaid with the Rx selectivity, and the T/I curve. Used to verify imports and as a sanity check before running interference analysis.
StoragePersisted to localStorage under ceragon_radio_library. Window geometry under lbc_window_radioModal.
A typical workflow: drop your .raf folder once, click 🧪 Derive Typical, then forget the library exists. The interference engine handles the rest.

📊 Interference Analysis (v8.1)

End-to-end C/I analysis across the whole network. Walks every designed link as a victim, finds all other links within a coordination radius, computes the aggregate interference, and reports per-link health (✓ healthy · ⚠ marginal · ✗ problem). Built around the INTERFERENCE_ENGINE module — the heaviest piece of the tool.

The workflow

  1. Import your antenna and radio libraries (one-time).
  2. Design the network — place sites, draw links, 🤖 Auto-Plan All Links.
  3. 🔗 Auto-bind interference setup — matches every designed link to its best radio (.raf) and antennas (.nsma / .adf) from the libraries, sets a centre frequency snapped to the BW raster, picks polarisations from the configuration (1+0 / 1+1 → V; 2+0 / 2+2 / 4+0 → V+H XPIC).
  4. 🎯 Allocate Channels — graph-coloring channel planner. Builds a conflict graph (links within the coordination radius sharing a band), runs DSATUR-greedy slot assignment to minimise co-channel collisions. Respects pinned channels (manualOverride.centreFreqMHz). Without this step every link sits at band-centre = co-channel by construction.
  5. 📊 Run Interference Analysis — prompts for a coordination radius (default 200 km), then computes C/I for every link. The radius caps how far the engine looks for potential interferers; very small radii under-count, very large radii waste time on negligible far-field contributors.

What the engine computes

For each victim link, the engine iterates over every other designed link as a candidate interferer, walks both ends of both links (4 victim×interferer end combinations), and for each combination evaluates every transmit×receive polarisation pair. Per-path interference power is computed as:

I = P_tx_max  − maskΔf  − off-axis_tx  − FSL  − diffraction  − atm  − XPD  − off-axis_rx  − NFD(Δf)
  • P_tx_max — max Tx power from the interferer's ADMOD (ITU-R F.758 convention).
  • maskΔf — Tx spectral mask attenuation at the frequency offset between interferer and victim. From the 81-point mask in the .raf file.
  • off-axis_tx / off-axis_rx — antenna RPE pattern attenuation at the geometric off-axis angle. Linearly interpolated between the .nsma/.adf data points; falls back to the ITU-R F.699-7 envelope outside the available range.
  • FSL — free-space loss (92.45 + 20 log d + 20 log f).
  • Diffraction — if the LOS engine reports the path is blocked or marginal, an additional knife-edge or smooth-earth diffraction loss is applied (ITU-R P.526 eq.31 for LOS-blocked, trans-horizon model for over-horizon).
  • XPD — 30 dB cross-pol penalty applied when transmit and receive polarisations differ, degraded by NFD when the carriers are off-channel.
  • NFD — Net Filter Discrimination from the T/I curve in the victim's .raf. Captures combined Rx selectivity + Tx mask rolloff at the Δf between carriers. Contributions with NFD > 80 dB are dropped as negligible.

The wanted signal C is computed at nominal Tx (also per F.758), and the required C/I comes from the victim radio's ADMOD row matching its design modulation. Margin = C/I_achieved − C/I_required. Verdict thresholds: healthy ≥ 6 dB, marginal 0–6 dB, problem < 0 dB.

Results card & map heatmap

A panel mounts under the Network toolbar with verdict counts at the top and a sortable per-link table below (worst margin first). Each row expands to show contributing interferers ranked by their I_dBm contribution; each contributor row shows geometry (off-axis angles, distance, LOS status), Δf and overlap class (cochannel / adjacent / off-channel), the budget breakdown, and per-pol pairs. Simultaneously, the map gets a colour-coded heatmap overlay — 6 px polylines on top of every analysed link, green/amber/red by verdict. Click any heatmap line or row to pan/expand to that link.

Per-link editing

The link popup gets an ⚙ Edit interference bindings button. Opens a modal with dropdowns for radio + antenna A + antenna B + centre frequency (BW-raster slots), and polarisation checkboxes per end. Each field has a 📌 Pinned / ◯ Auto toggle — pinned fields survive Auto-bind and Allocate Channels. ↺ Reset all to Auto clears every override and re-runs single-link auto-bind to repopulate from the libraries.

Mitigation suggestions

Expanding a problem or marginal link reveals 💡 Suggest fixes. The engine evaluates six candidate changes — swap polarisation at end A, swap at end B, swap both, shift one channel up / down, drop one modulation step — and reports each candidate's predicted new C/I margin (cached, so this is fast). Candidates that recover the link are marked ✓ Recovers, ones that improve but don't recover are ● Improves, neutral / worse are ✗ No help. Apply writes the change into the link's iface, pins the touched field (so Auto-bind won't undo it), invalidates the cache for that link, and re-runs the network analysis.

Persistence + export

Header buttons: 💾 Save bundles results + bindings + mitigation evaluations into a self-describing JSON (interference_TIMESTAMP_h{healthy}m{marginal}p{problem}.json); 📂 Load validates and re-opens a saved file, optionally restoring the bindings; 📄 Export PDF produces a multi-page report (cover + per-problem-link detail with contributors and mitigations + healthy appendix) via jsPDF (loaded on demand from CDN); 🔁 Re-bind runs Auto-bind across the network respecting pinned fields, then re-analyses.

Performance

The engine's _computeContribution calls are wrapped in a result cache keyed by an iface fingerprint (radio + antennas + centre freq + pols + site coords). Cap 50,000 entries with LRU eviction. Mitigation evaluation (6 candidates × full network walk per candidate) drops from minutes to seconds because only the changed link's pairs miss the cache — everything else is O(1) lookups. Cleared automatically by Apply Mitigation, Allocate Channels, Reset to Auto, and Dismiss.

Run sequence for a fresh network: Auto-Plan → Auto-bind → Allocate Channels → Run Analysis. After applying any mitigation, hit Re-bind to propagate the new channel choice to neighbouring links that might benefit. The whole loop fits in well under a minute even on 50-link networks.

📻 Radio settings

ControlWhat it does
BandMaster selector. Microwave appears first, followed by E-Band, W-Band and D-Band. Selecting Microwave reveals the MW-specific settings card; selecting a mmW band shows the mmW controls. Changing band resets frequency, bandwidth and product to defaults.
MW Product(MW mode only) Selects the Ceragon radio family, listed in deployment-preference order: IP-50CX (6–42 GHz wide-band, default), RFU-D (6–42 GHz), RFU-D-HP (4–11 GHz high-power, 10 GHz not supported), IP-50C (6–42 GHz wide-band). IP-20C-HP (4–11 GHz high-power) and IP-20C (6–42 GHz) are EOL and shown last for legacy reference. The product determines the available frequency bands, channel BWs, TX power and RSL thresholds.
MW Frequency BandNominal band in GHz (4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 11, 13, 15, 18, 23, 26, 28, 32, 38, 42 — availability depends on product). IP-20C uses 10.5 GHz internally for the 10 GHz band. IP-50C/CX and IP-20C do not support 4/5 GHz; RFU-D-HP supports only 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 11 GHz.
Channel BWChannel width in MHz. Available options depend on product, band and standard (ETSI / FCC). Some combinations are blocked where the product does not support them (e.g. IP-50C cannot use 224 MHz at 4/5/7/8 GHz).
mmW Product (E-Band)E-Band ships four product variants. Selecting one reconfigures the channel-bandwidth list and the configuration options (1+0 / 2+0 per BW):
  • IP-50E (default) — widest BW set (62.5, 125, 250, 500, 750, 1000, 1250, 1500, 1750, 2000 MHz). Only 250, 500, 1000 and 2000 MHz support 2+0; all other BWs are 1+0 only. RSL values in the datasheet are Guaranteed BER 10⁻⁶ (Typical = Guaranteed − 3 dB, BER 10⁻³ = corresponding BER 10⁻⁶ − 0.5 dB). IP-50E uses its own Dispersive Fade Margin (DFM) per modulation × BW; multipath outage uses the effective fade margin (FFM combined with DFM).
  • IP-50EX(A) — legacy standard variant, BW set 250 / 500 / 1000 / 2000 MHz. RSL values are Typical BER 10⁻⁶ (Guaranteed = Typical + 2 dB).
  • IP-50EX-P — same as IP-50EX(A) with TX power +3 dB.
  • IP-50EXA-P — same as IP-50EX(A) with TX power +6 dB (i.e. +3 dB above IP-50EX-P).
StandardETSI or FCC — affects channel BW options, EIRP assumptions and capacity frame size defaults.
Configuration1+0 = single carrier; 2+0 = two carriers (doubles capacity, adjusts branching loss); 1+1, 2+2, 4+0 also available where applicable. Note: when Multi-Band Aggregation is enabled and Band B is MW, this selector is restricted to 1+0 / 2+0 and Band A is auto-defaulted to 2+0 H-pol on entering that state.
RSL Performance LevelTypical = nominal published sensitivity; Guaranteed = worst-case conservative (lower fade margin).
RSL BER ThresholdBER point for the RSL threshold: 10⁻⁶ (stricter) or 10⁻³.
ACM & ACMBACM = Adaptive Coding & Modulation (link drops to a more robust profile during fades). ACMB (Adaptive Coding & Modulation Boost) extends the modulation ladder downwards by exposing BPSK 1/4 and BPSK 1/2 for maximum resilience under heavy rain. These two profiles are hidden from every UI surface (mod selector, performance table, chart, max-mod cap, exports) when ACMB is off, and re-appear automatically when ACMB is toggled on. Applies to all bands (MW + mmW E/W/D, including IP-50E). ACMB requires ACM to be on; it is blocked automatically when MW band is selected for products where it is not applicable.
PolarizationH or V. Vertical polarization suffers slightly less rain attenuation.

📡 Microwave-specific settings

When a Microwave band is selected a dedicated Microwave Link Settings card appears with the following controls:

Miscellaneous Loss (dB) — additional cable / connector / combiner losses (−10…+20 dB, default 0). Added on top of the standard branching loss.

Reliability Method — selects the multipath outage model. Three options:

  • Vigants-Barnett — classic North American model. Shows a C Factor panel with preset buttons (Mtn = 0.25, Avg = 1.0, Water = 4.0) and a free-entry numeric field. A second panel lets you enter a Climatic factor cf (Dry 0.5 / Avg 1.0 / Wet 2.0) and use the 🌎 Auto C from map button, which (i) estimates cf from the site coordinates (climate zone) and (ii) derives terrain roughness S from the loaded terrain profile, then computes C automatically using C = cf·(S/15.2)^−1.3.
  • ITU-R P.530-7/8 — international standard with path-type selector: Inland (Eq.29), Coastal / Large body of water (Eq.30) or Coastal / Medium body of water (Eq.31). For coastal types an Over-water fraction rc field (0–1) appears. The geoclimatic factor K is entered manually or auto-calculated (see below).
  • ITU-R P.530-17 — same K / εp inputs as P.530-7/8 but uses the updated distance exponent (d^3.6 vs d^3.0), giving more conservative results on longer paths.

Geoclimatic factor K (P.530-7/8 & P.530-17) — enter manually as a number or in scientific notation (e.g. 1e-4), or press the 📍 Auto button to calculate K from the pinned site coordinates using ITU-R P.530-7/8 Eq.29:

K = 5×10⁻⁷ · 10^(−0.1(C₀−CLat−CLon)) · PL^1.5

  • C₀: from the Lower Antenna Altitude (<400 / 400–700 / >700 m ASL) × Terrain Type (Plains / Hills / Mountains) lookup table (ITU-R P.530-7/8 Table 1). Auto-filled from terrain data when available.
  • CLat: 0 for |lat|≤53°; linear ramp to 7 at |lat|=60°.
  • CLon: +3 Europe/Africa; −3 Americas; 0 elsewhere.
  • PL: % of worst month with refractivity gradient <−100 N-units/km, approximated from latitude (tropical ~50%; sub-tropical ~30%; temperate ~20%; high-lat ~15%).

Path Inclination |εp| — auto-computed from antenna heights plus terrain end-point elevations: |ht−hr| / d (mrad). The hint line shows the computed value (e.g. Auto: 3.45 mrad). Enter a number to override manually; clear the field to revert to auto.

📈 Max-modulation caps (all bands)

The modulation list shown for each product is automatically capped to the maximum modulation that the product / channel BW / frequency band supports. All profiles from the lowest (BPSK 1/4 or BPSK / QPSK, depending on ACMB) up to and including the cap are shown; profiles above the cap are hidden.

The Max Modulation selector in the Modulation Performance table header lets you tighten the cap further (e.g. force the link to evaluate only up to 64 QAM regardless of what the radio can support). The selector applies to all bands, including E, W and D-band mmW products — the dropdown is populated from the product's currently valid modulation list and respects the ACMB filter (BPSK 1/4 / BPSK 1/2 hidden when ACMB is off).

🔭 Antennas

Pick a dish diameter for Site A and Site B. Larger dishes give higher gain (longer reach / more margin) but are heavier and have a narrower beam. The gain value used is shown next to the selector.

Additional Branching Loss accounts for losses in waveguide, couplers and combiners. A base value is set automatically from the configuration (1+0 vs 2+0 vs remote-mount); you can add extra dB on top.

Antenna heights (m AGL) default to 50 m above ground level for both sites. The Optimise Heights function computes the minimum heights for 60 % Fresnel clearance when terrain data is available.

🌧️ Path & Rain

ControlWhat it does
DistanceHop length. Set manually or auto-filled when both sites are pinned on the map.
📏 MaxPanel showing the maximum range for each modulation and target availability, given the current radio, antenna and rain settings.
Rain ZoneITU-R rain region (A–Q) or Manual for a custom rate. From Map uses the fetched ITU-R P.837-8 rain rate.
Target AvailabilityYearly uptime target (e.g. 99.995 % ≈ 26 min/yr downtime). The tool compares every modulation's predicted availability against this target.

🌍 Site Location & Climate (map)

Map toolbar (v8.2). The horizontal strip above the map carries every action that operates on the LBC link or map: 📁 File ▾ (Reports · LBC native Save / Load Design · Pathloss in / out · Add Current Link to Network), 👁 View ▾ (map background — Street / Satellite / Terrain / HOT / Blank), 🌐 Fetch Climate Data, and on the right the two destructive controls ✕ Clear Link and ✖ Clear highlights. The Network view has a symmetric toolbar above its own map host.

Drag confirmation: once a site marker has coordinates (set via input, loaded from a saved design, or brought in from the Network view), dragging it on the map prompts before committing the move — "Are you sure you want to move Site A to a new location?" / OK / Cancel. Cancel snaps the marker back to where the drag started; nothing downstream fires (no field updates, no terrain refetch). First-time placement (drop a pin where none exists) is not gated.

  • Pin sites — type a city in the Site A / Site B search box, or click on the map (1st click = A, 2nd = B). Distance appears automatically. Lat/lon can also be typed directly in the decimal fields, or switched to degrees-minutes-seconds with the DD⇄DMS toggle. The DMS fields accept multiple formats: 34.3803, 34° 22’ 49.2" N, 34 22 49.2 N, or N34 22 49.2.
  • ✕ Clear site — the red × button next to each site’s DD⇄DMS toggle clears all coordinate fields, removes the site marker, link line, terrain profile and climate data for that site.
  • Band switching — switching between Microwave and E/W/D-Band automatically clears the existing map link, terrain profile and climate data, since band parameters change completely.
  • ↑ Peak search radius — default 500 m. The ↑ Peak button finds the highest ground within this radius, useful for picking mast locations.
  • ↻ Terrain profile — fetches the elevation profile and draws the line-of-sight with the 60 % Fresnel zone. A Source selector offers six DEMs: Mapzen (default, opentopodata.org merged tiles — 10 m USGS NED in USA, 8 m LINZ in NZ, ~30 m SRTM-1 elsewhere; no key), Copernicus COP30 (OpenTopography.org, 30 m global TanDEM-X; requires a free API key), ASTER 30 m, EU-DEM 25 m, NED 10 m (USA), and SRTM 90 m (legacy). Sample count N is now adaptive (~one sample per 125 m, clamped to 60–400) so a 30 m DEM actually delivers obstruction-detection resolution on short links. If the selected source fails, the tool gracefully falls back through Mapzen → SRTM90 → open-elevation.com → open-meteo.com. The footer label under the profile shows the source that actually delivered the data.
  • ▲ Optimise Heights — auto-computes minimum antenna heights for 60 % Fresnel clearance. When Space Diversity is enabled, the optimiser also ensures the diversity (lower) antennas clear the terrain, accounting for the per-site separation.
  • 🌐 Fetch Climate — pulls temperature, pressure, humidity, water-vapour density and ITU-R P.837-8 rain rate for the site coordinates. This is the quick path: a single annual mean is fetched from Open-Meteo archive and a green badge appears with the four values.
  • ✅ Apply to Link Budget — pushes the fetched atmosphere and rain rate into the calculation.
  • 🌡️ Climate Explorer — below the badge sits a richer climate panel for engineers who want to design against a specific season or hour, not just the annual mean. See the next section — supports Annual, Month, Day and User select modes with click-to-pick hour selection on the 24-hour curve.

🌡️ Climate Explorer

The Climate Explorer is an optional override panel that sits directly below the existing Site Climate badge. Where the basic 🌐 Fetch Climate button gives you a single annual mean of T / P / WVD for Site A, the Climate Explorer lets you design against a specific month, a specific day down to the hour, or your own manually entered values. Same Site A coordinates — richer slice selection.

Opening the panel. The Climate Explorer is collapsed by default. Click the blue header bar or the ▼ Expand button to open it. On first expand it auto-pulls Site A coordinates and fetches the 30-year monthly climatology. You can also click 🌐 Load Site A at any time to refresh from the current coordinates — useful after you've moved the Site A pin on the map.

Data sources (in fallback order).

TierSourceDomainCoverage
Primary (monthly)NASA POWER 30-yr climatology (1994–2023) power.larc.nasa.gov 12 months × T mean / max / min, RH, surface pressure. Long-term averages — the right baseline for link design. Firewall-safe nasa.gov path.
Fallback (monthly)Open-Meteo archive (year-specific) archive-api.open-meteo.com Used if NASA POWER is blocked. Bucketises daily values from the previous calendar year into monthly means.
Primary (hourly, Day mode)NASA POWER hourly power.larc.nasa.gov Any date from 1981 to yesterday. 24 hourly values of T, RH, P. WVD derived via Magnus formula.
Fallback A (hourly)Open-Meteo forecast (past_days) api.open-meteo.com Used only when the requested date is within the last 90 days.
Fallback B (hourly)Open-Meteo archive archive-api.open-meteo.com Any date 1940 to yesterday — final fallback.
All three water-vapour densities are computed locally with the same Magnus formula used elsewhere in the tool: ρv = (216.7 · RH / (100·(T+273.15))) · 6.112 · exp(17.67·T / (T+243.5)), T in °C, RH in %, result in g/m³. Neither data source returns ρv directly.

Four override modes.

ModeWhat it appliesWhen to use
Annual (default) 12-month mean of T / P / WVD from the climatology. Identical behaviour to the legacy Fetch Climate + Apply path but using the 30-yr NASA POWER baseline rather than a single year. Standard long-term link planning. The default and the right choice unless you have a specific seasonal or diurnal design constraint.
Month Single month's mean T / P / WVD. The 6×2 month grid shows the WVD for each month so you can spot the worst-month at a glance. Click any month to apply it. Worst-month design for high bands (>20 GHz) where humidity drives gaseous attenuation. Pairs naturally with the existing P.841 worst-month ↔ annual conversion at the availability layer.
Day (hourly) Single hour's T / P / WVD from a specific calendar date. The full 24-hour curve is plotted (T on left axis, WVD on inner right axis, P on outer right axis, all clickable). Click any point on the curve to select that hour; the values card and the chart highlight update live. Worst-case design against a known weather event, or evaluation of how diurnal humidity / pressure variation affects a high-band link. The ⚡ Worst hour (max WVD) button auto-selects the 24-hour peak for the date you've chosen — typically the design-critical hour at 18 GHz and above.
User select Manual T (°C), P (hPa), WVD (g/m³) entry. Bypasses both NASA POWER and Open-Meteo entirely. Stress testing, sensitivity analysis, or matching a customer's published climate spec exactly (e.g. a tender that mandates ρv = 7.5 g/m³ standard atmosphere).

Hourly chart anatomy (Day mode).

  • Three curves rendered on one chart: orange T (°C), cyan WVD (g/m³), dashed purple P (hPa). Each curve has its own labelled axis in the matching colour.
  • Clicking anywhere along the curves selects the nearest hour. The green “Selected slice” card below updates with the exact triplet, and the chart highlights the WVD point at that hour.
  • ⚡ Worst hour (max WVD) — one-click selection of the hour with the highest water-vapour density, which is the design-critical hour for gaseous attenuation at high bands. Becomes enabled the moment hourly data finishes loading.
  • Date input accepts any value from 1940-01-01 to yesterday. Defaults to yesterday on page load.

Applying a selection. The ✅ Apply selected slice to Link Budget button becomes active as soon as you've made a valid selection. Clicking it:

  • Updates state.climate with the selected T / P / WVD;
  • Preserves the R0.01 rain rate if you'd already fetched it via the legacy path;
  • Tags state.climate.mode and state.climate.modeLabel so the PDF report and Save Design (.json) both record which slice was used — e.g. "2024-08-15 @ 14:00" or "July (monthly mean)";
  • Recolours the “Atmosphere” status line at the top of the modulation results card to show the active mode label and source in green.

Multi-band coupling. When Multi-Band Aggregation is enabled (e.g. Band A = 6 GHz + Band B = 23 GHz), both bands read the same state.climate — the atmosphere over a tower doesn't change with band. A single climate slice selected in the Explorer drives both link budgets. If you want Band A on annual and Band B on worst-month, use the existing P.841 worst-month conversion at the availability layer rather than feeding the two bands physically inconsistent atmospheres.

The legacy 🌐 Fetch Climate button still works as the fastest path for annual-mean design. The Climate Explorer is additive: a richer panel for the times you need to drill into seasonal or hourly variation.
FCC unit convention. When the Standard selector is set to FCC, all temperature displays in the Climate Explorer switch to °F automatically — chart axis titles, monthly chart curve, hourly chart curve, annual summary card, selected-slice card, and the User-select input label. Pressure stays in hPa and water-vapour density in g/m³ regardless of FCC mode, since both are international meteorological standards used the same way in US and European RF engineering. Internally, the value pushed into state.climate.T is always in °C — only the UI changes — so the ITU-R P.676 gaseous-attenuation formula receives the correct unit no matter which Standard is selected. Toggling FCC ↔ ETSI mid-session also converts any value you've typed into the User-select temperature field, so you don't lose your override.

⚙️ Defaults — Distance-Banded Planning Rules

The Defaults tab is the data source the auto-planner reads when it sizes every link in the network. A rule maps a distance band (From km / To km) to a Band, Product, Frequency, Channel BW, Target Capacity and Target Availability. When you run Auto-Plan, the link's geographic distance picks the matching rule, and the planner picks the configuration + reference modulation + antenna size that satisfy the rule.

→ Auto (cfg / mod) column (v8.1.15)

  • What it shows. The auto-resolved configuration (1+0 / 2+0 / 4+0 for non-protected Microwave; 1+1 / 2+2 for protected Microwave; 1+0 / 2+0 for mmW) and reference modulation that achieves the row's Target Capacity at the chosen BW + Product. Updates live whenever you edit Capacity, BW or Product.
  • How it's picked. Lowest configuration first — the planner tries 1+0, then 2+0, then 4+0 (Microwave) or 2+0 (mmW). At each configuration it takes the lowest modulation whose total capacity (per-carrier × carriers) meets the target. The chosen pair is the one Auto-Plan will use when it sizes a real link against this rule.
  • Reference modulation cap. The picker is capped at 256 QAM for Microwave and 64 QAM for mmW. Anything above the cap is too fragile to use as a planning reference under the design fade margin, so when the target can't be met within the cap at the current configuration, the loop escalates to the next configuration instead of locking in a high modulation. Worked example: E-Band 2000 MHz, target 13 000 Mbps → resolves to 2+0 / 32 QAM (15 600 Mbps), not 1+0 / 512 QAM.
  • Unachievable target. If even the largest configuration with the capped modulation can't deliver the target, the cell shows an amber ⚠ cfg · max. Either reduce Target Capacity, widen Channel BW, or accept that the planner will fall back to the highest available modulation at the largest configuration (and the link plan will carry a warning).

Bulk import (v8.1.15)

  • Import Rules from Excel or CSV. Click 📥 Import Rules in the Defaults toolbar. Each row in the file becomes one Distance-Banded rule. Expected columns: From (km), To (km), Band, Product, Frequency (GHz), Channel BW (MHz), Target Capacity (Mbps), Target Availability (%), Prot., SD. Headers are case-insensitive and accept reasonable spelling variants.
  • Normalisation. Band values like Microwave, MW and Microwave Band all map to the canonical Microwave Band. E-band, e, E Band all map to E-Band. Product names match case-insensitively and tolerate missing dashes (IP50EX → IP-50EX(A)). The Prot. column accepts Default, Defult (template typo), blank → null (use global); Yes, On, Protected, 1+1, 2+2 → forced protected; No, Off, Unprotected, 1+0, 2+0, 4+0 → forced unprotected. SD accepts Yes/No.
  • Preview before commit. Each parsed row is shown with the auto-resolved (config, modulation) so you can verify achievability before applying. Invalid rows (unknown band/product, BW not in catalogue, distance band reversed) are flagged and skipped. The amber warning banner reminds you that commit replaces all current rules.
  • Rules Template. Click 📎 Rules Template to download a ready-to-fill .xlsx with the eleven expected columns and 6 example rows (1 E-Band + 5 Microwave).

📊 Network Report (v8.1.21)

A multi-link summary report covering every link in the network. Opens from Network → File ▾ → 📊 Network Report. One row per link with the reference modulation result — configuration, polarization, capacity, antenna sizes (A + B), expected RSL, fade margin, rain and multipath outages, achieved availability against target.

Status colours

  • ✓ Meets target — achieved availability ≥ target.
  • ⚠ Marginal — within 0.5 % below target. Acceptable for low-priority links; worth a second look for high-availability paths.
  • ✗ Below target — more than 0.5 % below target. Needs a larger antenna, a more robust modulation, or a different configuration.
  • Draft — link exists in the network designer but hasn't been designed yet. Distance is shown (computed from site coordinates); all other cells blank. Run Auto-Plan or open the link in the LBC view to design it.
  • Compute error — the saved design state couldn't be re-evaluated. Hover the status pill for the exception detail.

Sortable columns

Click any column header to sort by that field (click again to reverse). Numeric columns sort by value, status sorts by severity (ok → marginal → below target → draft → error). Useful patterns: sort by Availability ascending to surface the worst-performing links first; sort by Status to group everything that needs attention.

Choosing columns (v8.1.22)

The ⚙ Columns button in the report toolstrip opens a dropdown panel where you can pick exactly which columns to show. Three presets are provided:

  • Compact — 7 columns: Link, Distance, Config, Ref Mod, Capacity, Availability, Status. Useful for at-a-glance scanning of large networks.
  • Standard — 23 columns. Default. Adds both sites' names and coordinates, antenna sizes, radio family details, and the full performance set (RSL, fade margin, rain/MP outages, target availability).
  • Detailed — 27 columns. Standard plus per-site antenna heights, Protection (Yes/No) and SD active (Yes/No). Useful when you need to verify the full design parameter set.

Picking individual checkboxes mixes any combination — the preset row drops the highlight and the layout is automatically saved as your "Custom" set. The Link and Status columns are always visible (locked) since they're the at-a-glance anchors for every row. Selection persists across sessions (browser localStorage).

Export to Excel honours your visibility choice — the Summary sheet contains exactly the columns shown on screen. The Per-modulation sheet is unaffected (always all modulations, always the same six columns).

Export to PDF (v8.1.23)

The 📄 Export to PDF button produces a multi-page landscape A4 PDF designed for review and distribution:

  • Cover page — project name, generation date, headline statistics (total links, meets-target / marginal / below-target / draft+error counts), aggregate capacity across all designed links at reference modulation.
  • Network Topology — sites and links drawn as native PDF primitives (no map rasterisation). Sites as navy dots with name labels; links colour-coded by status (green=meets, amber=marginal, red=below, grey dashed=draft, indigo=error). Auto-fits to the network bounds with a small padding margin. Legend on the right.
  • Summary Table — uses your currently-visible columns from the picker (Compact/Standard/Detailed/Custom). Status column coloured per row; numeric columns right-aligned. Coordinates are dropped here (too wide for landscape) — you'll see them on the per-link pages. Tip: choose Compact in the picker before exporting if your network has more than ~30 columns of detail you don't need in the printed view.
  • Per-Link Detail (one page per designed link) — three cards across the top (Site A · Geometry & Radio · Site B) with coordinates, antenna size and height, distance, band, product, frequency, BW, configuration, polarization, reference modulation. Below: the full per-modulation table with capacity, RSL, fade margin, rain/MP outages and availability at every modulation the radio supports. The reference modulation row is highlighted in navy bold so the user sees exactly which mod the report's headline figures came from. Draft links are excluded from this section (no design data).

Page footer on every page: project name · tool version · page X of Y. First click loads jsPDF + the autoTable plugin from CDN (cdnjs → jsdelivr → unpkg). Filename is LBC_Network_Report_<project>_<timestamp>.pdf.

Export to Excel

The 💾 Export to Excel button produces a two-sheet workbook:

  • Summary — one row per link with all default columns (same as the on-screen table).
  • Per-modulation — cross-table with one row per (link × modulation) pair, showing capacity, RSL, fade margin, rain/MP outages and availability at every modulation the radio supports at the chosen BW. Useful for ACM headroom analysis — see what capacity each step down buys you and at which margin it kicks in.

Filename is LBC_Network_Report_<project>_<timestamp>.xlsx when a project name has been set, else just the timestamp.

Phases: v8.1.21 shipped Phase 1 (summary table + XLSX). v8.1.22 added the column picker. v8.1.23 ships Phase 3 — multi-page PDF with cover, network topology diagram, summary table and per-link detail pages. Possible future addition: per-link path profile (terrain + Fresnel) on the detail page — held back from v8.1.23 because it would require async DEM fetches per link which slow the export down and can fail on a flaky network.

🔌 Space Diversity (SD)

From Defaults → Auto-Plan: the per-row SD checkbox in the Distance-Banded Planning Rules table enables Space Diversity at planning time. The checkbox is editable only for IP-20C-HP and RFU-D-HP products (the HP-class radios where SD is applicable). When ticked, every link the planner allocates against that rule is created with sdEnabled = true, IFC 3 dB combining, and a default 10 m antenna separation per side. The availability calculation in the planner already reflects the SD improvement; opening the link in LBC shows the SD card with the same values prefilled. The Defaults cascade forces SD off automatically if the row's product changes to a non-HP family.

Space Diversity places a second receive antenna vertically below the main antenna on the same tower. The two signals are combined digitally (IFC 3 dB combining), reducing the outage probability when ground-reflected multipath is the dominant impairment.

Eligibility: SD is available only for IP-20C, RFU-D, RFU-D-HP and IP-20C-HP at 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 10.5 and 11 GHz. The card is hidden for all other products and bands.
ParameterDescription
Enable SDToggle to activate SD improvement. When off the diversity antenna is not drawn and the I_SD improvement is not applied.
Antenna SeparationCentre-to-centre distance between main and diversity antennas, set independently per site (Site A / Site B). Default 12 m each. Displayed in feet when FCC mode is selected (internal calculations remain in metres). Asymmetric values are allowed (e.g. 10 m at Site A, 12 m at Site B) because ΔΦ A→B depends only on the Site B separation and vice versa. The Vigants I_SD uses min(S_A, S_B) conservatively.
Diversity Antenna SizeAntenna diameter for the diversity (lower) receive antenna at each site. Default Auto (1 size ↓) = one step smaller than the main antenna in the band's size list, clamped at the smallest. Select an explicit size to override. The gain difference ΔG = G(main)−G(div) reduces I_SD through the v² term. A hint below each selector shows the resolved size and ΔG penalty.
I_SD (improvement)Space diversity improvement factor applied to the multipath outage probability. Formula (Vigants, ITU P.530-8 Eq. 23):
I_SD = 1.2×10⁻³ × (f/d) × S² × v² × 10^(A/10)
where f = GHz, d = km, S = min(S_A,S_B) separation (m), v² = 10^(−ΔG/10) diversity-to-main gain ratio, A = flat fade margin for that modulation.
The SD Improvement badge shows the I_SD multiplier, diversity antenna sizes, and the v² penalty.
Diversity LOS statusA status badge next to the main LOS status. Shows ✓ Div LOS clear (with minimum clearance) or ⚠ Div LOS obstructed when the diversity line of sight enters the terrain. In that case, increase antenna heights or reduce separation.
Diversity antenna displayWhen SD is enabled the terrain profile shows the main antenna (blue/amber triangles) and the diversity antenna (green triangles) with per-site dashed vertical bars labelled with each site's separation.
Reflection analysis (◆)Shows the specular reflection point on the terrain for all four diversity path combinations, with the phase difference ΔΦ between the direct and reflected rays. The separation sweep table (6–15 m, 1 m steps) evaluates each separation independently for Site A and Site B, with colour-coded ΔΦ columns (≥150° good, 90–150° fair, <90° poor), ★ best per side, and independent Set A / Set B buttons. A recommendation banner shows the optimal per-site pair (which may be asymmetric) with an Apply Both button.
k-factor chartPlots the smooth-earth two-ray signal level vs the k-factor (0.3–15) for all four path combinations (using the per-site separations), showing how the earth's effective radius modifies the reflection geometry.

📡 Co-channel XPD / XPIC

Co-channel (same frequency, opposite polarisation) operation is used in 2+0, 2+2 and 4+0 configurations to double capacity in the same channel. The cross-polarisation discrimination (XPD) of the antennas and the optional XPIC canceller determine the carrier-to-interference (C/I) ratio. XPD/XPIC analysis is supported on all bands — MW at any CCDP configuration (2+0 / 2+2 / 4+0) and mmW (E, W, D) at 2+0; the same calculation chain (ITU-R P.530-8 / Vigants) is used, with the XPIF table taken from the Ceragon datasheet for the four 2+0-capable BWs (250, 500, 1000, 2000 MHz).

Availability: The XPD card appears whenever the configuration is a CCDP variant: 2+0, 2+2 or 4+0 for MW; 2+0 for mmW E/W/D. It is hidden for 1+0 and 1+1.

Inputs

ParameterDescription
Antenna XPD (dB)Minimum boresight XPD of the Tx and Rx antennas. Default 30 dB. Applied symmetrically.
XPIC deviceToggle on if an XPIC (Cross-Polarisation Interference Canceller) is fitted. The tool looks up the product-specific XPIF improvement factor per modulation and bandwidth from the Ceragon XPIF table.

Calculation chain (ITU-R P.530-8 / Vigants, K_XP = 0.7 single Tx)

StepFormulaNote
XPD₀XPDg+5 if XPDg≤35, else 40Adjusted antenna XPD (Eq. 33)
P₀pM × 10^(EFM/10)Fade occurrence factor (%)
η1−exp(−0.2·P₀^0.75)Multipath activity factor (Eq. 34)
Q−10·log₁₀(0.7·η/P₀)XPD multipath protection (Eq. 35)
CXPD₀ + QNet protection ratio under clear sky (Eq. 38)
C/I mph (no XPIC)−10·log₁₀(2·10^(−XPDg/10) + 10^(−(C−A)/10)) + 2Eq. 39; A = flat fade margin
C/I mph (+XPIC)−10·log₁₀(10^(−XPDg/10) + 2·10^(−(XPDg+XPIF)/10) + 10^(−(C+XPIF−A)/10)) + 2Eq. 40
XPD_RAIN15+30·log f − 12.8·f^0.19·log A₀.₀₁ (f≤20 GHz)
15+30·log f − 22.6·log A₀.₀₁ (f>20 GHz)		Rain-induced XPD degradation (Eq. 41)
C/I rain (no XPIC)−10·log₁₀(2·10^(−XPDg/10) + 10^(−XPD_RAIN/10)) + 2Eq. 42
C/I rain (+XPIC)−10·log₁₀(10^(−XPDg/10) + 2·10^(−(XPDg+XPIF)/10) + 10^(−(XPD_RAIN+XPIF)/10)) + 2Eq. 42 with XPIC

Interpreting C/I results: ≥20 dB Comfortable margin   10–20 dB Marginal — check with XPIC   <10 dB Interference dominated — XPIC required or link redesign.

XPIF lookup: the XPIF (XPIC improvement factor, dB) is looked up automatically from the Ceragon product table for the active product family and the nearest channel bandwidth entry. Three families are defined: IP-20C / IP-20C-HP (BW 3.5–80 MHz, QPSK–2KQAM), RFU-D / RFU-D-HP / IP-50C / IP-50CX (BW 3.5–224 MHz, BPSK–4KQAM), and mmW E/W/D (BW 250 / 500 / 1000 / 2000 MHz, BPSK 1/4–512 QAM — values from the IP-50E datasheet apply uniformly to E, W and D-band radios). BWs that do not support 2+0 (62.5, 125, 750, 1250, 1500, 1750 MHz under IP-50E) have no XPIF entry; the XPD card therefore only appears at the four 2+0-capable BWs even when IP-50E is selected.

📊 BTA — Backhaul Traffic Availability

BTA is the probability that the wireless link can carry the actual traffic offered by the RAN without congestion, per ETSI GR mWT 028 and ETSI TR 104 141. It is a stricter, more operationally meaningful figure than classical link availability:

  • Classical availability answers “what fraction of the year can the link sustain modulation X?” It compares fade margin to a single modulation threshold.
  • BTA answers “what fraction of the year can the link carry the offered traffic distribution?” It combines the ACM staircase (every modulation contributes its own capacity ceiling and its own outage time) with a statistical model of RAN traffic (peak Mbps + how bursty it is).
Why it matters: a link can spend hours per year operating on a reduced ACM profile that still carries the average RAN demand without dropping packets. Classical availability counts every step-down as a partial outage; BTA only counts intervals where the ACM ceiling drops below the actual traffic. The result is typically 1–3 percentage points higher than traditional availability — a more honest representation of user-perceivable QoE.

Two UI surfaces are provided, sharing the same underlying maths:

  1. Compact panel — an inline field labelled “📊 BTA — Backhaul Traffic Availability” in the main configuration area. Single toggle, peak (Mbps), an Avg/Peak ratio dropdown, and a live result tile. Use this for quick checks during planning.
  2. Detailed expandable card — near the bottom of the page, header “📊 BTA — Backhaul Traffic Availability (ETSI GR mWT 028)”. Adds the Lower Bound / Known Distribution mode selector, custom ratio entry, shape parameter k, an Auto-fill Peak button, a per-modulation contribution table and a breakdown badge. Use this for compliance reports and worst-case studies.

Inputs

ControlDescription
Enable BTA calculation(Compact panel only) Master toggle. The detailed card runs on demand via its ▶ Calculate button regardless. Default off.
BTA Mode(Detailed card only) Lower Bound — recommended. The tool searches over all valid Beta(α,β) distributions consistent with the chosen ratio f and CDF slope constraint (ETSI TR 104 141 eq.13), and reports the worst-case BTA. Useful when the true RAN traffic distribution is not known. Known Distribution — uses the specific Beta(α,β) implied by the chosen ratio and shape k. Use this when you have actual RAN traffic statistics.
Peak Traffic (Mbps)Maximum offered RAN traffic tmax. BTA measures how often the link can carry this peak — values above this are dropped (counted in Term A). The detailed card's Auto-fill Peak button sets this equal to the highest ACM capacity of the link, which represents the “link can never exceed its top mode” sanity case.
Avg / Peak Ratio (f)The burstiness parameter f = E[T] / tmax. Lower f ⇒ more bursty traffic ⇒ higher BTA (peaks are rare; the average is well below the ACM ceiling for most of the time). Presets:
  • 1/8 — very bursty (signalling, video keyframes)
  • 1/3 (c₃ = 3) — recommended conservative for single-site backhaul, per ETSI GR mWT 028
  • 1/2 — moderate (aggregated backhaul, mixed RAN)
  • 2/3 — heavy / sustained (large aggregation point, video streaming dominant)
  • 0.8 — very high utilisation (compact panel only)
  • Custom — free entry 0.05 – 0.99 (detailed card only)
Shape (k)(Detailed card, Known mode only) The shape parameter α of the Beta distribution. Typical RAN traffic 2–4 (default 4). Higher k ⇒ tighter distribution around the average; lower k ⇒ heavier tails. Ignored in Lower Bound mode (the search finds the worst-case shape itself).

How it's computed

The link is modelled as an ACM staircase with N steps, one per supported modulation profile. Each step has a capacity Cn and a Markov outage probability po,n (1 − availn/100), already computed by the main link-budget engine. The traffic T is a random variable on [0, tmax] with a Beta(α,β) PDF whose mean is f·tmax. BTA outage Prout is the sum of three terms:

  • Term A — P(T > CN): traffic exceeds the link's top-modulation capacity even when no fade is present. Pure capacity overflow.
  • Term B — bin contributions. For each interior modulation step n = 2…N, the bin [Cn−1, Cn] contributes (FT(Cn) − FT(Cn−1))·po,n. This is the probability that traffic falls inside that bin and the link is at or below the corresponding modulation due to fade.
  • Term C — FT(C1)·po,1: traffic is below the lowest modulation's capacity but the link is in total outage anyway.

BTA % = 100·(1 − Prout). In Lower Bound mode the tool sweeps α across 100 values with β = (1−f)/f·α and the CDF slope constraint from ETSI TR 104 141 eq.13, then returns the worst-case (lowest) BTA. The Beta(α,β) pair that wins the search is reported below the result.

Reading the result

BTAInterpretation
≥ 99.99 %Excellent — no perceivable QoE impact.
99.9 – 99.99 %Good — typical of well-dimensioned backhaul.
99.5 – 99.9 %Acceptable — minor congestion events possible during heavy rain.
99 – 99.5 %Marginal — increase Band A capacity, add Multi-Band, or accept QoE risk.
< 99 %Insufficient — link is undersized for the offered traffic distribution.
ETSI GR mWT 028 threshold: BTA ≥ 99.8 % is the published bound below which RAN users experience no perceivable QoE degradation. The detailed card flags this with a green check when met.

Per-modulation contribution table (detailed card only) shows, for each ACM step: modulation, capacity (Mbps), classical availability (%), outage po, P(T > Cn), the bin weight FT(Cn) − FT(Cn−1), and that bin's contribution to Prout. The largest contributions are highlighted in red; if Term A dominates the link is capacity-limited, if a middle bin dominates the link is fade-limited at that ACM step.

🔗 Multi-Band Aggregation (MW + mmW)

The Multi-Band Aggregation card lets you combine the main band (Band A, set in the top panel) with a second band (Band B) on the same path and present a single aggregate capacity-vs-availability curve. The typical use case is MW + mmW: an MW carrier provides the resilient backbone, while an E/W/D-band carrier delivers high additional capacity that gracefully steps down through its ACM profiles as rain attenuation rises.

Aggregation model: Each band runs an independent link budget (same distance, sites, rain, climate — separate radios, antennas, ACM tables, outage). Throughputs are summed at every availability level; Ceragon's proprietary L1 aggregation overhead (< 1 %) is ignored at this stage. MW and mmW outages are treated as independent — accurate to first order because MW (< 15 GHz) is multipath-dominated while mmW is rain-dominated.

Inputs

ParameterDescription
Enable MW + mmW capacity aggregationMaster toggle. When off the multi-band card is hidden and the main panel behaves as a single-band tool. Default off. While on, the 📊 Range Sweep Report is hidden because its sweep is Band A only and would not reflect the combined link.
Band A summaryRead-only line echoing the main panel's band, BW, polarisation, configuration and antennas, so both halves of the pair are visible side by side.
Band B — BandMW / E-Band (71–86 GHz) / W-Band (92–115 GHz) / D-Band (130–175 GHz). Any MW band can pair with any mmW band; MW×MW is also permitted.
Band B — Frequency / MW bandFor mmW: TX frequency within the chosen band (default 80 / 100 / 150 GHz). For MW: the MW frequency-band selector and product. The product list is ordered IP-50CX, RFU-D, RFU-D-HP, IP-50C, IP-20C-HP, IP-20C; the last two are EOL and kept for legacy reference.
Band B — E-Band PowerVisible only when Band B = E. Selects between IP-50E (default), IP-50EX(A) (standard), IP-50EX-P (+3 dB TX) and IP-50EXA-P (+6 dB TX) — same product set as the main panel. Band B BW options for IP-50E are still drawn from the legacy 250 / 500 / 1000 / 2000 MHz set (the wider IP-50E BW set 62.5–2000 MHz is available on Band A only in this release).
Band B — BandwidthIndependent channel BW for Band B. mmW: 250–2000 MHz from the mmW channel plan. MW: filtered by the current ETSI / FCC standard and the selected MW product — exactly the same list the main panel uses for Band A (e.g. IP-20C ETSI: 3.5 / 7 / 14 / 28 / 40 / 56 / 80 MHz; IP-50C FCC: 20 / 25 / 30 / 40 / 50 / 60 / 80 / 112 / 224 MHz). IP-50C / 50CX 224 MHz is excluded at 6, 7, 8, 13 GHz.
Band B — ConfigurationVisible only when Band B = MW. Options 1+0 / 2+0, default 2+0. Polarisation auto-sets to H for 2+0, V for 1+0. mmW Band B is fixed at 1+0 (SD / XPIC on Band B is out of scope for this release).
Band B — ACM / ACMBTwo toggles in the Band B Modulation Performance card header. Visible only when Band B is E / W / D (mmW). Independent of the main panel ACM / ACMB. ACMB requires ACM to be on, same rule as Band A; ACMB exposes BPSK1/4 and BPSK1/2 for maximum resilience under heavy rain.
Band B — PolarisationV / H, independent of Band A. Affects Band B's rain attenuation only.
Band B — AntennasSite A / Site B dish sizes. For mmW (E / W / D) the choices are restricted to the Ceragon mmW antenna line: 0.3 / 0.6 / 0.9 m in ETSI or 1 / 2 / 3 ft in FCC. For MW the full size list of the selected band applies, taken from the Ceragon antenna datasheet (gain looked up by band × size).
Use single dual-band antennaAvailable only when Band A is MW at one of 11, 13, 15, 18, 23, 28 GHz and Band B is E-band. When ticked, both bands share a single physical 2 ft dish per site whose gain at the MW frequency and at 70–80 GHz comes from the Ceragon dual-band antenna datasheet (table below). Both site antennas are locked to 2 ft (0.6 m).
Band A constraint when Band B = MW. Whenever Multi-Band is enabled and Band B is MW, the Band A configuration selector is restricted to 1+0 / 2+0 (the 1+1, 2+2, 4+0 options are temporarily removed). On entering this state, Band A is automatically defaulted to 2+0 with H polarisation, mirroring the recommended deployment when both bands run dual-carrier. The user can switch back to 1+0 afterwards; the auto-default only fires on the transition. Switching Band B to E / W / D restores the full Band A option set.

Band B branching loss is computed automatically from the Band B configuration:

  • MW Band B: looked up in the MW branching-loss table by configuration (1+0 / 2+0) and MW band, with cable loss added per remote-mount site. Same table as Band A — typical range ~0.6 dB (1+0 direct) to ~4 dB (2+0 high band).
  • mmW Band B (E / W / D): fixed at 1 dB (1+0).

Dual-band antenna gains (single 2 ft / 0.6 m dish, MW + E-band 70–80 GHz):

MW bandDish sizeMW gainE-band gain (70–80 GHz)
11 GHz2 ft / 0.6 m34.4 dBi50.0 dBi
13 GHz2 ft / 0.6 m35.4 dBi50.0 dBi
15 GHz2 ft / 0.6 m37.0 dBi50.0 dBi
18 GHz2 ft / 0.6 m38.0 dBi50.0 dBi
23 GHz2 ft / 0.6 m40.0 dBi49.0 dBi
28 GHz2 ft / 0.6 m42.0 dBi50.0 dBi
If Band A is moved to an MW band without a dual-band antenna variant (6 / 7 / 8 / 32 / 38 GHz), or Band B is switched to W or D-band, the dual-band option auto-disables and the antenna selectors unlock. Two separate antennas are then required at each site.

Outputs

OutputDescription
Headline tilesFour tiles showing aggregate Mbps at 99.99 %, 99.999 %, 99.9999 % and at the user's current availability target. Each tile breaks out the A and B contributions in the second line.
Per-band summary tableOne row per band with frequency, BW, polarisation, Gt / Gr, FSL, rain A0.01, top modulation, top capacity, outage at top mode and availability at top mode.
Capacity-vs-availability chartLog-nines X axis (99 % → 99.99999 %), throughput on Y. Three stepped curves: Band A (dashed), Band B (dashed), Aggregate (solid filled). A vertical red dashed line marks the current target availability. Scroll = zoom · Drag = pan · ⟲ Reset zoom.
Band B Modulation Performance tableSeparate card appearing below the Band A table. Same 12 columns (Modulation, TX, RSL Thr., Unfaded RX, Fade Margin, Rain / MP / Total outage, Time in Mod, Availability, Status, Capacity), populated from the Band B compute. Column widths are locked identical to the Band A table so the two align row-for-row. Header shows the Band B identifier and reliability method (ITU-R P.530-17 for mmW; whichever MW method is selected). The Capacity column honours the global Capacity Frame Size selector and, for MW Band B 2+0, doubles by the carrier multiplier.
Band B antenna gains in 📊 Link Budget DetailTwo additional rows appear in the Antennas column of the Link Budget Detail card: Gain Tx (Site A) — Band B and Gain Rx (Site B) — Band B, each showing the dBi value and the dish size used for Band B. Hidden when Multi-Band is off.
Headlines change tiles & chart labelsWhen Multi-Band is on, the top-of-page “🔴 Capacity vs Availability” and “📈 Distance vs Availability” charts add a · Band A only suffix to their titles, because those charts are single-band; the aggregate view lives in the Multi-Band card below.

Reading the chart

  • At low availability targets (left of chart) both bands operate at top mode, so aggregate = MW peak + mmW peak.
  • As the target moves right (more nines), the mmW curve drops first — rain fade pushes it through lower ACM profiles, eventually to zero.
  • At very high availability targets the aggregate collapses toward MW-only — the resilient floor.
  • The cross-over point (where the aggregate equals MW + mmW vs MW alone) is the rain-fade margin of the mmW carrier.
For most planning work, the 99.999 % tile answers “what aggregate capacity can I commit to for a 5-nine SLA?” in one number.

PDF Report: when multi-band is enabled, the generated PDF adds an extra page (after page 4) with the headline table, per-band configuration row, dual-band antenna note (if active), and the rendered capacity-vs-availability chart.

Limitations & assumptions: MW and mmW outages are summed as independent processes (rain-cell correlation across this frequency span is < 1 % effect for typical hops); the L1 aggregation overhead (proprietary Ceragon, < 1 %) is treated as zero; the path geometry (terrain, K-factor, multipath inputs) is shared between bands. Out of scope for Band B in this release: Space Diversity, XPD / XPIC co-channel analysis, and configurations beyond 1+0 / 2+0 (mmW Band B is fixed at 1+0). When Band B = MW + 2+0 the carrier multiplier doubles the per-mode capacity per the MW carriers table.

📋 Reading the results

The status panel at the top shows the best modulation that meets the availability target, the associated capacity, RSL, fade margin and outage seconds. The Modulation Performance table lists every modulation profile with TX power, RSL threshold, flat and effective fade margin, rain outage, multipath outage, combined availability and capacity. Rows are colour-coded: green = meets target, amber = within 0.5 %, red = below target.

📋 Tables & charts

  • Capacity vs Availability — each point is the real availability of a modulation at the current link distance.
  • Distance vs Availability — availability vs distance for every modulation. The red dashed line is your target; the navy dashed line is the current distance. X-axis options: 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100, 150 km. Y-axis: 95 % – 100 % on a log-outage scale; curves flatten and pin to the 100 % gridline when outage approaches zero. In FCC mode, X-axis labels, dropdown options, and the chart note automatically switch to miles.
  • ETSI / FCC units — toggling the standard switches all distance, height and separation values across the entire tool: distance (km ↔ mi), antenna heights (m ↔ ft), SD separation (m ↔ ft), terrain profile, reflection table, and all chart labels. Internal calculations always use SI units.

💾 Export & reporting

Where to find these (v8.2): the LBC view's reports and design-save actions live in 📁 File ▾ on the map toolbar above the Site Location & Climate map — a Reports section (Export to Excel, PDF Report) followed by LBC native (Save / Load Design), Pathloss interchange (Import / Export Pathloss), and the Single-Link bridge (Add Current Link to Network). The global header's File ▾ carries actions that span the whole network (sites, network file, KML interchange, libraries).

  • Export to Excel — saves the full link budget, modulation table and distance/availability sweep to an .xlsx workbook with colour-coded formatting.
  • 📄 PDF Report — generates a professional multi-page PDF report (Ceragon branded) containing: site & link parameters (page 1), modulation performance & capacity table with colour-coded availability (page 2), terrain profile chart (page 3, if coordinates are pinned), and distance vs availability chart (page 4). All values adapt to the current ETSI (km/m) or FCC (miles/ft) unit system. The jsPDF library loads on-demand from CDN when the button is first clicked.
  • 💾 Save Design — saves the entire link configuration to a portable .json file for record-keeping or to revisit the planning later. The file captures everything needed to reproduce the design: radio settings (band, product, frequency, bandwidth, configuration, polarization), antennas and mounts, ACM/ACMB toggles, XPIC/XPD, propagation method and all P.530-19 / P.841 / Vigants-Barnett parameters, Space Diversity (per-site separation), distance, rain rate, target availability, BTA, frame size, modulation selectors, multi-band aggregation (Band B + dual-band antenna), and the site coordinates with antenna heights. A small dialog collects optional metadata (link name, site names, project, designer, notes) used for the filename and stored inside the file as a record. The designer name is remembered between sessions. Esc cancels, Enter saves.
  • 📂 Load Design — opens a previously saved .json design file and restores every setting end-to-end: state is rebuilt, all dropdowns and toggles are synced, multi-band is re-enabled if applicable, site coordinates are restored and the map markers are re-pinned. The link line is redrawn and the full link budget is recomputed automatically. Files saved by older LBC versions remain loadable — a console note flags the version mismatch but values are restored on a best-effort basis (missing fields fall back to current defaults).
  • Range Sweep — Export — exports the generated capacity vs distance sweep table to a colour-coded .xlsx workbook.
  • ☾ / ☀ toggle in the header switches between light and dark themes.
Design file format. Save Design writes a small, human-readable JSON document (typically a few KB) tagged with _format: "ceragon-lbc-design", a _formatVersion (currently 1.0), the LBC tool version that produced it, and an ISO 8601 timestamp. The file can be archived alongside the PDF report, attached to a project ticket, edited by hand in any text editor if needed, or committed to version control. Filenames are auto-built from the link name (or site names, or coordinates) plus the save date: LBC_<name>_YYYY-MM-DD.json.
Use Save Design at any milestone of the planning — after the initial sizing, after agreeing on antenna sizes with the customer, or before a band/product change — so you can always come back to a known-good baseline by loading it.

📖 Methodology & references

RecommendationUsed for
ITU-R P.530-17Multipath fading model (default MW method). Also used for rain outage probability formula (§2.3.1).
ITU-R P.530-18Rain path reduction factor (frequency-dependent). Base: d0 = 35·exp(−0.015·R). P.530-18 applies a frequency correction χ(f) ≥ 1: d0,eff = d0/χ(f), deff = d/(1+d/d0,eff). χ=1.0 below ~10 GHz (same as P.530-16); χ=1.145 at 19 GHz; χ=1.777 at 37 GHz; χ=2.583 at 80 GHz. Higher χ → shorter effective rain path → less predicted attenuation → better availability at medium and long distances. Significant improvement over P.530-16 for all bands ≥ 19 GHz, especially E-band (70/80 GHz).
ITU-R P.530-19Latest (09/2025) multipath fading model, selectable for MW links. Uses the integral digital products LogK.csv and dN75.csv (0.25° global grids, embedded in this tool) with the new regression Eq.(7) and the all-percentages interpolation of §2.3.2. Worst-month results optionally converted to annual via ITU-R P.841.
ITU-R P.530-7/8Earlier multipath model, selectable for MW links.
ITU-R P.838-3Specific rain attenuation coefficients k and α.
ITU-R P.837-8Rain rate R₀.₀₁ from map coordinates.
ITU-R P.676-12Gaseous (O₂ + H₂O) atmospheric attenuation.
ITU-R P.835Standard reference atmosphere (when no site climate is fetched).
NASA POWER30-year monthly climatology (1994–2023) and historical hourly data for any date from 1981 to yesterday. Primary data source for the Climate Explorer (monthly + hourly modes). Public service of NASA Langley Research Center — power.larc.nasa.gov.
Open-MeteoYear-specific archive + forecast reanalysis. Fallback data source when NASA POWER is unreachable. Also the source of the elevation profile (4-API fallback chain) and the basic Fetch Climate button's annual mean.
Magnus formulaWater-vapour density from temperature and relative humidity: ρv = (216.7·RH/(100·(T+273.15)))·6.112·exp(17.67·T/(T+243.5)). T in °C, RH in %, result in g/m³. Used because neither NASA POWER nor Open-Meteo returns ρv directly.
ITU-R P.453Reference for PL (refractivity gradient maps) used in K calculation.
